title: PayPal (PYPL) Intrinsic Value
description: An independent discounted cash flow (DCF) intrinsic value analysis of PayPal (PYPL) compiled by Gemini.

fcfGrowth.title: FCF Growth Rate (Y1-Y5)
fcfGrowth.rationale: A highly conservative 5% growth rate is assumed. This reflects a mature business facing intense competition, where top-line growth is sluggish and margin expansion is challenging. It relies primarily on the natural growth of digital payments rather than market share gains.

discountRate.title: Discount Rate (WACC)
discountRate.rationale: A 10% discount rate accounts for the competitive threats from Big Tech and the transition risks associated with the new management team's strategic pivot.

terminalGrowth.title: Terminal Growth Rate
terminalGrowth.rationale: A 2% terminal growth rate assumes PayPal will grow roughly in line with long-term inflation, acting as a stable, utility-like fixture in the global digital economy.

thesisTitle: Priced for Terminal Decline, Delivering Solid Cash Flow
thesisParagraphs: The market has aggressively repriced PayPal from a high-flying fintech disruptor to a legacy financial utility in terminal decline. At current valuations, the stock is trading at a mid-single-digit multiple to its free cash flow, an incredibly pessimistic scenario that implies the company's business model is completely broken. While competition from Apple Pay and others is fierce and transaction margins are under pressure, the reality is that PayPal remains a highly profitable, cash-generating machine with a sticky, two-sided global network.

Our DCF model demonstrates a massive margin of safety. Even if PayPal's free cash flow grows at an anemic 5% annually—a hurdle it can likely clear simply through global e-commerce expansion and cost-cutting—the intrinsic value is more than double the current share price. The company's aggressive stock buyback program, funded by its $5 billion+ annual FCF, acts as a powerful catalyst. By retiring shares at these distressed levels, PayPal is structurally increasing the per-share value of its cash flows, making it an extraordinarily compelling deep value opportunity.

faq.question: Is Apple Pay going to destroy PayPal?
faq.answer: Apple Pay is certainly taking market share on mobile, but PayPal maintains dominance on desktop e-commerce and possesses a massive, loyal user base. The payments market is large enough to support multiple players.

faq.question: Why is the calculated margin of safety so high?
faq.answer: The market has applied a severely compressed multiple to PayPal due to growth concerns. Our DCF model focuses purely on the present value of the cash the business generates, which remains incredibly robust.

faq.question: What happens if PayPal fails to grow at 5%?
faq.answer: Even with zero growth in free cash flow, the mathematical intrinsic value would still sit significantly higher than the current market price, highlighting the extreme undervaluation currently present.
